---
title: "Pineland Heliotrope (Euploca polyphylla)"
slug: "euploca-polyphylla"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474778207/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Pineland Heliotrope"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Boraginales"
tax_family: "Boraginaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Boraginales", "Boraginaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474778207/medium.jpg" alt="Pineland Heliotrope" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/marktancig">
marktancig</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264337488">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Boraginales
-   **Family:** Boraginaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=769874)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and small white flowers arranged along curved, coiling stems. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as pine rocklands and coastal uplands, particularly in southern parts of the state. It grows during the warmer months and often occurs as a subtle component of groundcover in open, sun-exposed areas.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/769874-Euploca-polyphylla)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-euploca-polyphylla/)
